companies.name: Vizzly

traction.peak_metric: 80 sign-ups on initial MVP (pre-pivot); unknown customer count at acquisition

death_analysis.killer_competitor: Looker (Google), Explo, open-source Apache Superset
death_analysis.death_mechanism: Vizzly built an embeddable analytics platform for SaaS companies using JS web components instead of iFrames, offering superior customization for customer-facing dashboards. The initial MVP attracted 80 sign-ups but suffered 100% churn within the first week, triggering a pivot to the embeddable analytics positioning. With only $500K from YC S22 and a 4-person London team, Vizzly competed against Looker (acquired by Google for $2.6B), Sigma Computing, and pure-play rivals like Explo that were better capitalized. The company's Twitter account never posted a single tweet, its LinkedIn reached only 568 followers, and it never raised a follow-on round despite operating for 2 years post-YC. In Q4 2024, Gain Theory (a WPP-owned consultancy) acquired Vizzly in what appears to be an acqui-hire, with competitors immediately soliciting Vizzly's orphaned customers.
death_analysis.critical_decision_point: After the 100% first-week churn on the initial MVP in April 2022, Vizzly pivoted to embeddable analytics but kept opaque pricing and a self-serve motion without the developer community investment to make it work. They should have immediately adopted transparent, public pricing and invested their limited resources into a single high-visibility developer channel (e.g., an open-source SDK component) to build the inbound pipeline that 568 LinkedIn followers and zero tweets could never generate.

similar_that_survived: Explo - survived in embedded analytics with better capitalization and actively absorbed Vizzly's customers post-acquisition
Metabase - survived with open-source community distribution and enterprise upsell model

companies.one_liner: 80 sign-ups, 100% first-week churn, acqui-hired after $500K

companies.name: CodeStory

traction.peak_metric: #1 on SWE-Bench Verified at 62.2% resolution rate; announcement tweet generated 143,000 views and 559 reposts

death_analysis.killer_competitor: Cursor (raised $60M Series A in August 2024, then $900M Series B) — same VSCode-fork approach but executed on distribution and fundraising while CodeStory optimized for benchmarks
death_analysis.death_mechanism: CodeStory built Aide, an AI-native IDE forked from VSCode, achieving #1 on SWE-Bench Lite at 43% and later #1 on SWE-Bench Verified at 62.2% resolution rate using only Claude Sonnet 3.5. Despite these genuine technical milestones, the company raised only $500K from YC and never secured follow-on funding, while direct competitor Cursor raised a $60M Series A and then a $900M Series B in the same period. The team pivoted three times in 20 months — from chat/autocomplete to multi-file agentic editing to AgentFarm background agents — each time resetting the user acquisition clock and preventing compounding growth. With only 200+ repositories onboarded and 20+ daily professional users, the benchmark leadership never converted into commercial traction, and CodeStory shut down in February 2025 roughly 10 weeks after its highest-visibility moment.
death_analysis.critical_decision_point: In early 2024, the founders pivoted away from chat and autocomplete, concluding the UX was 'limiting to the real abilities of LLMs.' This was the moment that sealed CodeStory's fate. Had they stayed with the chat/autocomplete paradigm — the same product category Cursor was scaling — and used their benchmark leadership to raise a $5-10M seed round, they could have competed on distribution rather than ceding the entire product category to a better-funded rival.

similar_that_survived: Cursor - same VSCode fork approach but raised $960M+ and prioritized user growth over benchmarks, reaching massive adoption
Codeium (Windsurf) - survived by building an AI coding assistant with enterprise focus and raising $150M+
Sourcegraph (Cody) - leveraged existing code intelligence platform to add AI coding features with built-in enterprise distribution

companies.one_liner: #1 on SWE-Bench with $500K while Cursor raised $960M — benchmarks don't bank

companies.name: Vizly

death_analysis.killer_competitor: OpenAI Code Interpreter (free with ChatGPT Plus); Microsoft Copilot for Excel/Power BI
death_analysis.death_mechanism: Vizly built an AI-powered BI tool that let users query and visualize data in plain English, with on-premises deployment for data privacy, supporting GPT-4, Claude 3.5 Sonnet, Snowflake, and BigQuery. The 2-person team raised $500K from YC S23 and never secured follow-on funding. OpenAI's Code Interpreter launched in July 2023 — the same month as Vizly's YC batch — offering similar functionality free to ChatGPT Plus subscribers. Microsoft embedded Copilot in Excel and Power BI, and Google launched Duet AI for Sheets. Vizly's $19.99-$29.99/month consumer pricing couldn't fund the enterprise sales motion that its on-premises differentiator required, and the company shut down quietly in 2024 when co-founder Ali Shobeiri joined Perplexity.
death_analysis.critical_decision_point: After launching the on-premises Mac app with Llama 2 in September 2023, Vizly should have abandoned the consumer pricing entirely and gone all-in on enterprise sales with $500+/seat annual contracts targeting regulated industries (healthcare, finance) that legally couldn't use cloud AI tools. The on-premises architecture was a genuine moat for that segment, but consumer pricing at $29.99/month signaled the wrong buyer.

similar_that_survived: ThoughtSpot - survived in natural language BI by raising $600M+ and building deep enterprise sales infrastructure
Julius AI - survived by focusing on the prosumer segment with aggressive free-tier growth and community building

companies.one_liner: $30/mo tool vs. free OpenAI Code Interpreter on $500K

companies.name: Remade

traction.peak_metric: 250,000+ LoRA downloads on Hugging Face; 'millions of generations' powered

death_analysis.killer_competitor: Adobe Firefly and Canva AI (absorbing generative capabilities into platforms with tens of millions of existing users, plus fal itself commoditizing the infrastructure layer from below)
death_analysis.death_mechanism: Remade built an AI-native canvas for video ad workflows targeting lifestyle brands, later expanding to context-aware generation, character consistency, inpainting, 4K upscaling, and real-time collaboration for up to 50 users. The 4-person Cambridge CS graduate team released 8 Wan2.1 video LoRAs on Hugging Face that accumulated 250,000+ downloads and became some of fal's most popular endpoints — demonstrating engineering value while undermining their own product's defensibility by accelerating infrastructure commoditization. Commercial traction was thin: ScentBird was the only named paying customer, and the claimed 130% higher clickthrough rates came exclusively from the founders' own YC launch post with no independent verification. fal acquired Remade on November 6, 2025 — approximately 12-15 months after founding — in its first-ever acquisition, absorbing the team and model assets rather than a customer base or revenue stream.
death_analysis.critical_decision_point: Instead of open-sourcing the 8 Wan2.1 LoRAs that became fal's most popular endpoints, Remade could have kept them proprietary as the core differentiation layer of its canvas product — using exclusive video effects as a moat to drive enterprise adoption. The open-source strategy generated 250K+ developer downloads but zero enterprise contracts, effectively giving away the team's primary technical asset and making the standalone product replicable.

similar_that_survived: PhotoRoom - achieved significant consumer and SMB distribution through its mobile app before expanding into professional workflows, winning on distribution first and depth second
Runway ML - raised $230M+ for AI-native video generation by maintaining proprietary models and building a creative-professional brand rather than open-sourcing core assets

companies.one_liner: 250K LoRA downloads, 1 paying customer, acquired by fal in 12 months

companies.name: deepsilicon

death_analysis.killer_competitor: Etched (raised $120M for transformer-specific ASIC) and open-source tools llama.cpp and bitsandbytes that offered free quantization with community support, making Deepsilicon's paid software layer a hard sell
death_analysis.death_mechanism: Deepsilicon built software and planned hardware for running transformer neural networks faster using ternary quantization — compressing model weights from 16 bits to 2 bits for claimed 5x RAM reduction and 20x throughput improvement. The company raised only $500K from YC (S24) with a 2-person team, but pursued a full-stack hardware-software co-design strategy that typically requires $100M+ and multi-year timelines — competitors Etched raised $120M and Groq raised hundreds of millions for similar chip ambitions. The software layer, intended as the near-term revenue engine, competed against free open-source tools (llama.cpp, bitsandbytes, ExLlamaV2) while Microsoft's BitNet b1.58 paper published the core ternary quantization technique publicly, eroding any proprietary moat. The company's last public activity was its Hacker News launch in September 2024; co-founder Alexander Nanda subsequently joined Mercor, and YC listed the company as inactive after an operating life of roughly 6 months.
death_analysis.critical_decision_point: At founding in 2024, the team chose a full-stack hardware-software co-design strategy instead of focusing exclusively on the software optimization layer. Had they scoped the company purely as a software inference optimization startup — abandoning the ASIC roadmap entirely and focusing on building the best commercial ternary quantization toolkit with reproducible benchmarks and enterprise support — the $500K could have funded 12+ months of pure software development with a viable path to Series A via demonstrated enterprise adoption.

similar_that_survived: Groq - survived by raising hundreds of millions before attempting custom AI chip production, with a functioning LPU delivering record inference speeds
Etched - survived by raising $120M specifically for transformer ASIC development with a larger engineering team and fab relationships
Together AI - thrived by focusing on the software/cloud inference layer without custom silicon, raising $300M+ to optimize model serving

companies.one_liner: $500K to build custom ASICs — competitors had $120M and a 3-year head start

companies.name: Beek

traction.peak_metric: 4M+ total users, 250K+ audiobooks, 18x paying subscriber growth over 2 years, 8x YoY revenue growth

death_analysis.killer_competitor: Audible (Amazon) — acquired Beek's exclusive Originales catalog
death_analysis.death_mechanism: Beek built the largest Spanish-language audio content platform, growing from an emoji-based book review app (2M+ monthly visits, 250K MAUs, 200K reviewed titles) to a $12/month audiobook subscription with 4M+ users and 250K+ audiobooks. The company raised ~$20M total including a $13M Series A led by Lightspeed in February 2023, with angels including Sam Altman and Dylan Field. Beek correctly identified a massive gap — 486M native Spanish speakers but only ~50K audiobooks vs. hundreds of thousands in English — and built a creator marketplace where 70%+ of new users came from creators and 78% listened to at least one original monthly. But its most defensible asset, the exclusive 'Beek Originales' catalog, was also the asset Audible (Amazon) most wanted. After the Series A, Audible acquired a significant portion of the Beek Originales catalog, absorbing the supply Beek had spent years building. The startup proved the market existed; the incumbent with superior distribution captured it.
death_analysis.critical_decision_point: Before or during the February 2023 Series A, Beek should have negotiated long-term exclusive creator contracts with lock-up periods and penalties to make the Originales catalog acquisition-proof, or pursued a direct acquisition by Spotify or Amazon at a premium while the catalog's strategic value was at its peak. Instead, the catalog was absorbed piecemeal by Audible without a full company acquisition that would have rewarded investors.

similar_that_survived: Storytel — Swedish audiobook platform that survived by expanding into 25+ markets with a licensing-first model less vulnerable to single-market catalog absorption
Podimo — Danish audio subscription platform raised $100M+ and built exclusive creator relationships with longer lock-up periods across European markets

companies.one_liner: 4M users, 250K audiobooks built for Audible to absorb

companies.name: Bicycle AI

traction.peak_metric: 11 employees across two countries

death_analysis.killer_competitor: Intercom, Drift, and Zendesk Answer Bot (pure-software chatbots with zero marginal labor cost)
death_analysis.death_mechanism: Bicycle AI built a human-supervised AI system to handle Level 1 customer support tickets, entering YC W17 with $120K and 11 employees split between San Francisco and Bengaluru. The core problem was that 2017-era NLP — pre-GPT, reliant on rule-based systems and intent classifiers — simply could not automate enough tickets to beat traditional BPO unit economics. If the AI handled only 30% of tickets autonomously, the human supervision cost made the service more expensive than outsourcing, not less. A team member wrote publicly during the YC batch that working there "made me sober up from the AI and chatbot hype." With $120K supporting 11 salaries (even at Bengaluru rates, runway was measured in weeks), the company attempted a desperate pivot to a trivia quiz app, then returned remaining capital to investors and shut down by August 2018. Both co-founders went on to co-found Appsmith, which raised $51.5M and hit a $250M valuation — confirming the failure was timing, not talent.
death_analysis.critical_decision_point: In mid-2017 after YC Demo Day, the founders could have raised a seed round to extend runway and repositioned as a pure BPO service with an AI augmentation layer — proving unit economics first, then gradually increasing automation as NLP improved. Instead, with only $120K and 11 employees, they had no margin for iteration and shut down within 18 months.

similar_that_survived: Intercom Fin — waited until GPT-4 era (2023) to launch AI support, now handles 50%+ of tickets
Ada — raised $190M for AI customer support, survived by launching after transformer models existed
Forethought — raised $92M, succeeded by building on modern LLMs rather than 2017-era NLP

companies.one_liner: $120K ÷ 11 employees = weeks of runway × pre-GPT NLP

companies.name: Breaker

traction.peak_metric: 67% one-month retention rate (beta); 700,000 shows indexed; 4,210 App Store ratings

death_analysis.killer_competitor: Apple Podcasts (pre-installed on every iPhone) + Spotify ($1B+ podcast acquisitions of Anchor, Gimlet, Parcast)
death_analysis.death_mechanism: Breaker built a social podcast app where users could see what friends were listening to, like episodes, and comment — launching on iOS in March 2017 with a 67% one-month retention rate among beta users. The core thesis was that friend-driven discovery could solve podcasting's discoverability problem, but the social features required network density that never materialized: after nearly 4 years, Breaker had only 4,210 App Store ratings versus Anchor's 83,000 and Audible's 3.2 million. The app was iOS-exclusive for over 2 years (Android beta arrived August 2019), directly constraining the social graph density it needed. Podcast listening proved too solitary and asynchronous — people listen while commuting, cooking, running — to support the social engagement frequency that makes social apps sticky. In January 2021, Twitter acqui-hired the team to build Twitter Spaces, and Maple Media bought the app assets in a 72-hour negotiation, then immediately stripped every social feature and relaunched it as a reskin of Player FM — the most concise verdict that the social layer had no standalone value.
death_analysis.critical_decision_point: In 2017-2018, Breaker should have launched on Android simultaneously with iOS rather than waiting over 2 years until August 2019. For a social app whose value depends entirely on network density, excluding the majority of global smartphone users for 2+ years guaranteed the social graph would remain too sparse to make the core feature compelling.

similar_that_survived: Spotify — solved podcast discovery by cross-promoting to 400M+ music subscribers rather than building a standalone social layer
Overcast — survived by focusing on audio quality features (Smart Speed, Voice Boost) rather than social, building loyal power-user base
Pocket Casts — survived via premium features and eventual acquisition by Automattic, never depended on social network effects

companies.one_liner: 4,210 ratings in 4 years vs Audible's 3.2M = acqui-hired

companies.name: Clear Genetics

traction.peak_metric: 67,000+ patients used Gia with 92% satisfaction; deployed at Geisinger, Huntsman Cancer Institute, and Johns Hopkins Hospital

death_analysis.death_mechanism: Clear Genetics built Gia, an NLP-powered genetic counseling chatbot deployed at Geisinger (250,000 patients sequenced) and Huntsman Cancer Institute, raising only $2.52M in seed funding. Rather than failing, the company hit a distribution ceiling inherent to enterprise healthcare sales: scaling beyond a handful of marquee health system deployments required either a much larger sales organization or a platform partner. Invitae, already a paying customer running Gia in its direct channel, acquired Clear Genetics in November 2019 for approximately $50M (~20x return on capital), converting a go-to-market constraint into a strategic exit after just 3 years of operation with fewer than 50 employees.
death_analysis.critical_decision_point: In 2019, the founders could have raised a Series A to build their own enterprise sales team and pursue independent scale beyond their handful of health system deployments. Instead, they accepted Invitae's $50M acquisition offer, which was rational given the $2.52M capital base but capped the outcome. Had they raised $10-15M and built direct sales, the 67,000+ patient user base and Johns Hopkins clinical validation could have supported a $200M+ independent company in the genomics workflow space.

similar_that_survived: Invitae - the acquirer itself scaled to $500M+ revenue by aggregating genetic testing and workflow tools, though it later faced its own financial challenges
Genome Medical - survived by building a broader telehealth genetic counseling platform with $63M+ in funding
Color Health - thrived by focusing on population-scale genetic testing programs for employers and health systems, raising $278M+

companies.one_liner: $2.5M in, $50M out in 3 years: the 20x exit that looked like a win

companies.name: FloydHub

traction.peak_metric: 100,000+ users; 2,500 users within 1 month of launch; 6,000 waitlist at Demo Day

death_analysis.killer_competitor: AWS SageMaker (launched November 2017) and Google Colab (free GPU notebooks, launched 2017) -- together they eliminated FloydHub's abstraction advantage and undercut its pricing
death_analysis.death_mechanism: FloydHub built a 'Heroku for Deep Learning' platform that let data scientists train and deploy models without configuring GPU environments, growing to 100,000+ users over five years with only $120K in YC funding and a 4-person team. The product delivered genuine value -- 2,500 users within one month of its February 2017 launch and 6,000 on the waitlist -- but the business was a margin play reselling AWS reserved GPU instances. AWS SageMaker launched in November 2017, just 8 months after FloydHub's debut, offering managed ML infrastructure bundled into existing enterprise cloud agreements. Google Colab launched the same year as a free alternative. FloydHub's user base of individual researchers and students -- the hardest segment to monetize in infrastructure -- never converted to paying customers at a rate that could sustain the business, and the founders shut down in August 2021 stating they 'couldn't build a sustainable business.'
death_analysis.critical_decision_point: In 2018, after reaching significant organic growth, FloydHub could have pivoted aggressively toward enterprise teams with an on-premises offering and dedicated sales motion, rather than continuing to serve individual practitioners on a usage-based model. The on-premises tier was listed at Demo Day but never received dedicated investment -- pursuing enterprise contracts at $50K-$100K/year could have generated sustainable revenue before hyperscalers absorbed the individual segment.

similar_that_survived: Paperspace - direct competitor that survived by raising more capital and eventually being acquired by DigitalOcean in 2023
Weights & Biases - pivoted from a consumer ML tool to enterprise experiment tracking, raised $250M+, and built a defensible MLOps niche
Lambda Labs - survived by selling GPU cloud compute focused on AI training, raised $600M+ by specializing in H100 infrastructure

companies.one_liner: 100K users loved it, but free Colab and AWS SageMaker killed the margin

companies.name: Niles

financials.revenue_at_death: ~$196K (unverified, from Latka database — may be cumulative or annualized)

traction.peak_metric: ~$196K revenue (unverified Latka figure); Apartment List enterprise reference customer; 2 TechCrunch features in one week

death_analysis.death_mechanism: Niles built a Slack-native knowledge bot that let employees ask questions and receive answers drawn from chat conversations and documents, addressing the universal problem of internal wikis that no one uses. The product earned TechCrunch coverage twice in one week at YC W17 Demo Day, secured an enterprise reference customer (Apartment List), and launched a 4-tier SaaS model from $25/month to custom enterprise pricing. But the NLP technology available in 2017-2019 — pre-BERT, pre-GPT — was not reliable enough to deliver accurate answers from unstructured conversational text, and a wrong answer from a knowledge bot trains users to stop asking questions entirely. With only $120K in total funding (the standard YC check) and no follow-on round, the 2-person team could not invest in the ML engineering needed to close the accuracy gap. Niles shut down around early 2020, and co-founder Mack Lu joined Snowflake.
death_analysis.critical_decision_point: Niles attempted to build fully automated question-answering from unstructured Slack conversations using 2017-era NLP. Had the team instead implemented a human-in-the-loop model — routing unanswered questions to designated team experts and using their responses to build a structured knowledge base — the product could have delivered reliable answers immediately while accumulating training data for future automation, buying time until better NLP models arrived.

similar_that_survived: Guru — direct competitor in Slack-integrated knowledge management that raised $40M+ and survived long enough to relaunch with GPT-4-powered AI features in 2023
Glean — AI-native enterprise knowledge search that raised $200M at a $1B valuation in 2022 by launching after transformer models made the core NLP problem tractable

companies.one_liner: Slack knowledge bot, $120K raised — NLP wasn't ready until GPT-3

companies.name: Penny

traction.peak_metric: 300,000+ downloads

death_analysis.killer_competitor: Mint (Intuit-backed, free, dominant personal finance app with massive brand recognition and marketing spend)
death_analysis.death_mechanism: Penny built a conversational personal finance app using a chat-based interface to help users track spending and manage bills across 10,000+ financial institutions via Plaid. The company raised $1.32M total ($1.2M from Social Capital in May 2016 and $120K from YC W17), reaching 300,000+ downloads — impressive capital efficiency at roughly $0.004 per download. But Penny had no monetization model and was competing for consumer attention against Mint (free, backed by Intuit's multi-billion-dollar balance sheet) in a category where the viable revenue path — financial product referrals — required scale Penny hadn't yet achieved. By March 2018, the team was about to begin Series A fundraising when Credit Karma (80M members) made an acquisition offer. With no revenue, a crowded market, and a funding cliff approaching, the founders accepted the acqui-hire — and within 6 months, Credit Karma launched a hard inquiry notification experience using Penny's conversational platform that drove a 10%+ increase in member satisfaction.
death_analysis.critical_decision_point: After reaching 300,000 downloads by mid-2017, Penny could have immediately implemented financial product referrals — recommending credit cards and loans based on spending patterns via the conversational interface — rather than deferring monetization entirely. Even modest referral revenue ($5-10 per conversion on 300K users) would have provided a revenue story to support Series A fundraising and reduced the pressure that made the Credit Karma acqui-hire the only rational exit.

similar_that_survived: Credit Karma — survived and sold to Intuit for $8.1B by building the financial product referral model at scale that Penny never implemented, with 100M+ members generating billions in referral revenue
Cleo — survived as a conversational finance app by raising $300M+, launching in the UK first for lower competition, and building subscription revenue alongside a credit-building product

companies.one_liner: 300K downloads on $1.32M, but $0 revenue vs free Mint

companies.name: Pit.AI

financials.revenue_at_death: $0 (carry-only model with no AUM ever deployed)

death_analysis.death_mechanism: Pit.AI built a reinforcement-learning-based hedge fund that aimed to replace human trading hypotheses and eliminate the standard 2-and-20 fee structure, charging only carry with zero management fees. At YC W17 Demo Day in March 2017, the models were running on paper with no real money deployed, and the founder projected live trading within 1 year — a projection that was never met across 4 years of operation. The carry-only model meant the company earned $0 in revenue until it could raise LP capital, deploy it, and generate positive returns, but institutional allocators require 12-24 months of audited live returns before committing. With only $120K in seed funding from YC and Zillionize, a 2-person team, and a Twitter account (@PitAITech) that published 0 tweets and had 39 followers, Pit.AI quietly closed in early 2021 without ever launching a live fund.
death_analysis.critical_decision_point: Instead of operating as an actual hedge fund requiring LP capital and regulatory infrastructure, Pit.AI could have built and licensed its RL-based strategy optimization as enterprise software to existing quant funds — a SaaS model that would have generated recurring revenue from day one without requiring AUM, audited track records, or SEC compliance. Numerai's crowdsourced tournament model proved that an AI-native finance company could generate revenue and attract capital without directly managing LP money.

similar_that_survived: Numerai - built a crowdsourced hedge fund model that sidestepped the LP fundraising problem by using a crypto-token incentive structure, raising $50M+
Quantopian (partially) - pivoted from a hedge fund to licensing algorithms before being acquired by Robinhood in 2020

companies.one_liner: Raised $120K to beat Renaissance Tech; published 0 tweets in 4 years

companies.name: Rally Tennis

death_analysis.killer_competitor: USTA League Programs (decades of brand recognition, existing club relationships, and a built-in player base at every tennis club in America)
death_analysis.death_mechanism: Rally Tennis entered YC W17 with a single-sentence pitch — 'Join tennis league and play competitively with people nearby' — and attempted to solve the hyper-local cold-start problem inherent to participation-dependent sports marketplaces. The product required a critical density of players at compatible skill levels in each specific geographic area to deliver any value, but with only the standard YC $120K investment and no follow-on funding, the company had no capital to seed multiple cities simultaneously. Rally Tennis was not mentioned in TechCrunch's coverage of the W17 Demo Day (which covered 52 companies across 2 days), suggesting zero investor interest post-presentation. The company went dark in 2017, leaving no shutdown announcement, no app reviews, and no user counts — its YC profile URL was later repurposed for a different company (Zestful), an unusual outcome suggesting early dissolution.
death_analysis.critical_decision_point: Instead of trying to build a standalone app requiring critical mass in multiple cities, Rally Tennis could have partnered directly with 5-10 tennis clubs in a single metro area (e.g., San Francisco), offering the app as a free league management tool for club pros — pre-seeding supply through institutional relationships rather than trying to attract individual players cold. The 2021 Rally Tennis that later built the same concept to 20,000 players across 150 cities proved the market demand was real, but required years of city-by-city expansion effort.

similar_that_survived: Rally Tennis (2021 entity, unrelated) - built the same concept to 20,000+ players across 150 cities by launching post-COVID when U.S. tennis participation surged 22% (2019-2021)
Meetup.com - survived as a general-purpose local activity platform by aggregating across hundreds of interest categories rather than going vertical into a single sport

companies.one_liner: 0 press mentions at Demo Day; needed 52 cities, had $120K

companies.name: Soomgo

financials.revenue_at_death: ~$24M USD (2022 annual revenue; post-2022 figures undisclosed)

traction.peak_metric: 31.4B KRW (~$24M USD) annual revenue (2022); 120 employees; 30,000+ service providers; 160%+ YoY revenue growth (2021)

death_analysis.killer_competitor: Kakao and Naver's latent platform threat suppressed strategic acquisition premium
death_analysis.death_mechanism: Soomgo built South Korea's broadest horizontal local services marketplace covering 1,000+ categories -- from piano lessons to interior design -- growing to 31.4 billion KRW (~$24M USD) in annual revenue by 2022 with 120 employees and 30,000+ service providers. Despite raising $43-57M across 6 rounds and reportedly reaching a $500M+ valuation, the company hit a structural ceiling: horizontal breadth in a single 52-million-person national market limited growth vectors, and neither Kakao, Naver, nor Coupang stepped forward as a strategic acquirer at an acceptable price. In September 2024, Korean PE firm Ark & Partners completed a buyout at an undisclosed price -- a liquidity-over-upside outcome likely closer to Dealroom's $114-172M estimate than the $500M valuation, leaving venture investors with a moderate return after a decade of operation.
death_analysis.critical_decision_point: After the $28.6M Series C in June 2021 with 160%+ YoY revenue growth driven by COVID tailwinds, Soomgo could have used the momentum to expand into Japan or Southeast Asia where similar horizontal marketplace gaps existed. Instead, the company focused domestically and doubled headcount, leaving it trapped in a single national market when growth decelerated post-COVID. International expansion during the 2021 funding window could have unlocked a venture-scale outcome rather than a PE buyout.

similar_that_survived: Thumbtack -- raised $700M+ and focused exclusively on the US market but with a population 6x Korea's, providing a larger TAM ceiling
Bark.com -- survived in the UK local services market by raising less capital and setting expectations for a smaller outcome

companies.one_liner: 10 years, $24M revenue, $500M peak -- sold to PE for less

companies.name: Tress

traction.peak_metric: 60,000 users across 90+ countries; 30,000 weekly active users at Demo Day

death_analysis.killer_competitor: Instagram (existing behavior already happening on the platform)
death_analysis.death_mechanism: Tress built a vertical social network for Black women to discover and share hairstyles, launching on Android in Nigeria in February 2016 and attracting 60,000 users across 90+ countries with 30,000 weekly active users and 20% week-over-week growth at YC W17 Demo Day. But the company raised only $120K total — from MEST and YC combined — and chose an ad-dependent monetization model that required millions of MAUs to generate meaningful income. With zero revenue 10 months after launch, an Android-only product that blocked access to higher-value Western ad markets, and Instagram's network effects making it nearly impossible to pull users to a second platform, Tress silently shut down in May 2018 with no public explanation.
death_analysis.critical_decision_point: In early 2017, after hitting 60,000 users with strong engagement, Tress should have pivoted from ad-dependent monetization to a marketplace model connecting users with local hairstylists for booking and payment, charging a 10-15% transaction fee. This would have generated revenue from hundreds of transactions rather than requiring millions of ad impressions, and would have created a network effect Instagram couldn't replicate.

similar_that_survived: StyleSeat - survived as a salon booking marketplace by monetizing transactions between stylists and clients rather than relying on ad revenue

companies.one_liner: 60K users, $120K funding, $0 revenue — ads need millions

companies.name: Openland

traction.peak_metric: 250+ communities; largest single community reached 14,000 members and 500,000+ messages

death_analysis.killer_competitor: Discord (100M+ registered users with free unlimited tier, expanding aggressively from gaming into creator and professional communities)
death_analysis.death_mechanism: Openland raised $2.37M from 30 investors including Gagarin Capital, Sinai Ventures, Soma Capital, and YC on a real estate marketplace thesis, then pivoted to build a horizontal community messenger competing directly against Slack (12M DAU), Discord (100M+ registered users), and Facebook Groups (1.8B monthly users). The team of 14 spent 18-24 months in private beta building a feature-complete product with voice calls, group chats, and automation, launching publicly in January 2020. Post-launch, 250+ communities signed up and the largest reached 14,000 members with 500,000+ messages, but a third-party analysis in 2022 found virtually no active communities remaining — severe engagement decay across the platform. The company removed its 'Featured Communities' section in January 2021, a signal that showcase-worthy activity had evaporated, and shut down in April 2022 having generated no disclosed revenue.
death_analysis.critical_decision_point: Instead of pivoting away from the real estate marketplace in 2018 — before testing it with paying customers — Openland could have stayed in the vertical where incumbents had no foothold. A dedicated communication and deal-flow platform for urban land development stakeholders (owners, builders, investors, brokers) could have built network effects in a specific niche before expanding, rather than competing head-on against Slack and Discord with $2.37M.

similar_that_survived: Circle — survived by targeting paid creator communities with native monetization tools, raising $29M and embedding into the creator economy stack
Geneva — survived by focusing on group-chat-first community for Gen Z organizers, raising $24M with a mobile-native design that avoided the Slack/Discord comparison
Mighty Networks — survived by combining courses, community, and membership into a single creator platform, raising $50M+

companies.one_liner: 250 communities launched, 0 active at death, $2.37M spent

companies.name: Reverie Labs

traction.peak_metric: Roche/Genentech multi-target collaboration (July 2020); 4 pipeline candidates including RVL-101 at IND-enabling stage

death_analysis.killer_competitor: Recursion Pharmaceuticals and the broader biotech funding winter of 2022-2023
death_analysis.death_mechanism: Reverie Labs built an AI-powered drug discovery platform that pivoted twice -- from pharma SaaS to molecules-as-a-service to fully integrated drug developer -- after discovering the pharma software TAM was tens of millions, not billions. Despite raising $32M and landing a marquee Roche/Genentech collaboration in 2020, the pivot to drug development introduced 5-to-10-year clinical timelines structurally incompatible with venture capital return horizons. The company burned through its $25M Series A at an estimated $8-12M/year, and with lead candidate RVL-101 still at IND-enabling stage by May 2023, it could not raise a Series B. In February 2024, Ginkgo Bioworks acqui-hired 4 team members for $3.53M in RSUs -- a fraction of the $32M raised -- effectively ending Reverie as an independent company.
death_analysis.critical_decision_point: After the Roche/Genentech deal in July 2020, Reverie chose to become a fully integrated drug developer rather than remain a platform licensing ML-designed candidates. Had they stayed asset-light and licensed their kinase inhibitor designs to multiple pharma partners, they could have generated milestone revenue from several programs simultaneously without the $8-12M/year burn of running their own pipeline, potentially reaching Series B profitability milestones before the 2022 biotech downturn.

similar_that_survived: Recursion Pharmaceuticals -- raised over $1B and built proprietary high-throughput biology infrastructure, providing capital cushion through the biotech downturn
Insitro -- stayed platform-focused with Gilead partnership rather than becoming a drug developer, preserving optionality

companies.one_liner: Raised $32M to cure cancer with AI; acqui-hired for $3.53M

companies.name: Sqreen

traction.peak_metric: 800+ customers; ~$7.9M estimated revenue; Greylock-led $14M Series A; 85-120 employees

death_analysis.killer_competitor: Datadog (the acquirer) and observability platform convergence with security
death_analysis.death_mechanism: Sqreen built an in-app security microagent that detected and blocked attacks at runtime, growing from 0 to 800+ customers including Algolia, BlaBlaCar, and Toptal on $18M in funding over 6 years. The product was technically superior to legacy WAFs -- Greylock's Sarah Guo issued a term sheet within 4 days of meeting the CEO -- but the architecture that made it great (an in-app agent surfacing security telemetry) was identical to what observability platforms like Datadog already deployed at scale across 14,000+ customers. When Sqreen began its Series B fundraise in 2020, estimated revenue of ~$7.9M and a customer growth rate of just 60% over 22 months (500 to 800) fell short of the $15-25M ARR bar. Datadog acquired Sqreen in April 2021 for a reported ~$220M, absorbing the technology into Datadog Application Security Management -- a strong return on $18M raised, but an acknowledgment that standalone application security had become a feature, not a company.
death_analysis.critical_decision_point: When Sqreen opened its US office in 2019 after the $14M Series A, it could have aggressively pursued enterprise accounts with $100K+ ACV contracts rather than continuing its developer-led, self-serve motion that produced ~$10K average revenue per customer. Shifting to enterprise sales earlier could have pushed ARR toward the $15-25M threshold needed for a strong Series B, potentially enabling Sqreen to remain independent and compete as a platform rather than being absorbed as a feature.

similar_that_survived: Contrast Security -- raised $200M+ and survived by focusing specifically on enterprise RASP/IAST with a sales-led motion targeting larger contracts
Snyk -- raised $1B+ in developer security by expanding beyond runtime protection into the full software development lifecycle

companies.one_liner: 800 customers, $18M raised, $220M exit -- became a feature

companies.name: Station

traction.peak_metric: 40,000 weekly active users; 6 hours/day average usage; 600+ app integrations; Product Hunt Product of the Year

death_analysis.killer_competitor: Ferdium and open-source alternatives (Franz, Rambox) that replicated the product for free; Arc browser absorbing the use case natively
death_analysis.death_mechanism: Station aggregated 600+ SaaS web apps into a single Electron-based desktop interface with universal search and unified notifications, reaching 40,000 weekly active users spending an average of 6 hours per day -- winning Product Hunt's Product of the Year and attracting users at Uber, Atlassian, Airbnb, and Spotify. But the product launched free and stayed free for its entire 4-year commercial life: the team acknowledged in October 2017 it was 'working on a paid offering for teams' but never shipped one. The planned B2B2B monetization model -- selling promotional placement to SaaS vendors -- required distribution leverage that 40,000 free users could not provide. With $3.25M in seed funding, ~$800K-1M/year burn, 9 employees, and zero revenue, the company quietly wound down around 2021 and open-sourced the codebase. Users migrated to Ferdium, a free open-source alternative, confirming the need was real but unpayable.
death_analysis.critical_decision_point: When Station had 2,500 highly engaged weekly active users at launch in October 2017 -- each using the product 6 hours/day -- the team should have immediately launched a paid team tier at $10-15/user/month rather than pursuing free growth. At that engagement level, even a 10% conversion rate on 2,500 users would have generated $30-45K/month in recurring revenue, validating willingness to pay before the free-product expectation became entrenched across 40,000 users who never expected to pay.

similar_that_survived: Notion -- launched free but built collaborative features that justified a $10/user/month team plan, creating enterprise revenue that funded independence
Raycast -- desktop productivity launcher that launched with a free tier but quickly introduced a paid Pro plan, avoiding the free-forever trap

companies.one_liner: 40,000 users, 6 hrs/day, $0 revenue -- open-sourced to die

companies.name: Storyline

traction.peak_metric: 5,000+ published Alexa skills (8%+ of entire Skills Store); 3,000 users; Kids Court skill won Amazon's $25K Grand Prize

death_analysis.killer_competitor: Amazon's own improving developer console and the failure of the Alexa Skills ecosystem to achieve consumer adoption
death_analysis.death_mechanism: Storyline built a drag-and-drop tool that let non-technical users create Alexa skills without code, publishing over 5,000 skills -- more than 8% of the entire Alexa Skills Store -- in just 13 months with 3,000 users. But the Alexa Skills ecosystem never developed meaningful consumer engagement: users stuck to native functions like music, timers, and weather, leaving Storyline's hobbyist user base structurally unwilling to pay for a $49/month Pro tier. A November 2018 pivot to Invocable -- a $99/month voice UX prototyping tool for professional designers -- solved monetization in theory but introduced fatal churn: prototyping is episodic, not recurring. Invocable shut down on April 15, 2019, just 5 months after launch and 18 months after founding, on total funding of $770K. The displaced user base overwhelmed competitor Voiceflow's beta, forcing it into early general availability.
death_analysis.critical_decision_point: When Sayspring was acquired by Adobe in April 2018 -- validating voice prototyping tools as acquirable assets -- Storyline could have immediately pivoted to enterprise voice UX design rather than waiting until November 2018 to launch Invocable. Those 7 lost months burned through a significant portion of the $770K total funding. An earlier pivot would have given Invocable more runway to iterate on retention before the April 2019 shutdown, and Adobe's acquisition interest might have extended to Storyline if it had been positioned as an enterprise tool sooner.

similar_that_survived: Voiceflow -- absorbed Storyline's displaced users, raised $3.5M CAD in April 2019, and pivoted to enterprise conversational AI design to survive the voice platform winter
Otter.ai -- built on voice technology but targeted transcription (a proven, recurring need) rather than voice app creation (an unproven, episodic one)

companies.one_liner: Built 8% of Alexa's skills store; died when nobody used them

companies.name: Allo

traction.peak_metric: 100%+ week-over-week growth during YC batch (Jan-Mar 2019)

death_analysis.killer_competitor: Nextdoor (245,000+ neighborhoods, 27M MAUs by 2019)
death_analysis.death_mechanism: Allo was a 2-person mobile app for neighboring families to exchange non-monetary favors (babysitting, carpools, pet-sitting) via a karma-based reciprocity system. During YC W19, the San Francisco pilot reported 100%+ week-over-week growth — but from an extremely small absolute base (likely dozens of users, not thousands). The product faced a textbook cold-start problem: it needed neighborhood-level density to be useful, but couldn't attract density without already being useful. With only $150K from YC covering 3-6 months of SF operating expenses, the team couldn't sustain the bootstrapping period needed to reach critical mass. No follow-on funding was raised after Demo Day on March 22, 2019. Nextdoor's 27M MAUs and 245K neighborhoods, plus entrenched Facebook parent groups, made the non-transactional karma model impossible to pitch to investors.
death_analysis.critical_decision_point: At YC Demo Day in March 2019, rather than pitching a non-transactional karma-based model with no clear revenue path, Allo should have introduced a paid tier or transactional component (e.g., paid babysitting marketplace) that would have given investors a clear monetization thesis and enabled the company to raise the $1-2M seed needed to sustain neighborhood-by-neighborhood density building.

similar_that_survived: Nextdoor — solved the same neighborhood connectivity problem but with a broader use case and advertising-based revenue model
Care.com — monetized the family services exchange directly through paid listings and background checks

companies.one_liner: 100%+ W/W growth from ~20 users vs Nextdoor's 27M MAUs

companies.name: Allure Systems

traction.peak_metric: $1.4M ARR at YC Demo Day (March 2019), ~$3M revenue at acquisition, 14% conversion rate lift

death_analysis.death_mechanism: Allure Systems is not a failure story — it was acquired by Farfetch (NASDAQ: FTCH) on December 20, 2021, in a cash transaction. The company built AI-generated on-figure fashion photography, entered YC W19 with $1.4M ARR and a 14% conversion rate lift for retail clients, and grew to approximately $3M in revenue with a 17-23 person team. Founded in 2017 by serial entrepreneur Gabrielle Chou (two prior exits) and computer vision specialist Jeremy Chamoux, the company raised only ~$3.7M total — a capital-efficient path to acquisition. The timing was strategic: the deal closed before the 2022-2023 generative AI wave commoditized AI image generation, meaning Farfetch acquired proprietary technology when it was still scarce.
death_analysis.critical_decision_point: The acquisition by Farfetch in December 2021 was well-timed — had the company waited and tried to scale independently into 2022-2023, the generative AI wave (Stable Diffusion, DALL-E, Midjourney) would have commoditized their core technology and eroded their competitive position. Selling before the market crowded was the right call.

similar_that_survived: PhotoRoom — raised larger rounds and expanded into broader AI image editing beyond fashion
Vue.ai — pivoted from fashion-specific AI to broader retail automation and survived through diversification

companies.one_liner: $1.4M ARR + 14% conversion lift = Farfetch acquisition in 4 yrs

companies.name: Basilica

death_analysis.killer_competitor: Google BERT (free, Oct 2018) + Hugging Face (free model hub) + OpenAI embeddings ($0.0001/1K tokens)
death_analysis.death_mechanism: Basilica built a paid embedding API that converted images and text into vectors, allowing developers to build AI classifiers with ~1,000 labeled examples instead of 1 million. The fatal timing: Google released BERT for free in October 2018, one month before Basilica's November 2018 Show HN launch. Hacker News commenters immediately flagged BERT and GloVe 2B as free alternatives on launch day. With only ~$150K from YC W19, a 2-3 person SF team burning $30-40K/month had 3-5 months of runway. The competitive clock accelerated relentlessly: Hugging Face's transformers library (2019) made BERT trivially accessible, Sentence-BERT (2020) competed directly with Basilica's text embeddings, and OpenAI's embedding API (2022) offered superior quality at $0.0001 per 1K tokens. Basilica's Python client last updated in December 2019, just 9 months after Demo Day, and no follow-on funding was ever raised.
death_analysis.critical_decision_point: At YC Demo Day in March 2019, rather than positioning as a general-purpose embedding API competing with free models, Basilica should have immediately specialized in a single high-value vertical (e.g., medical imaging, legal document classification) where domain-specific fine-tuning could justify a premium over generic free embeddings. The general-purpose positioning made them directly comparable to BERT and every subsequent free model.

similar_that_survived: Cohere — raised $445M+ by building enterprise-focused embedding APIs with compliance, SLAs, and fine-tuning that justified paying over free alternatives
Pinecone — pivoted from embeddings to vector database infrastructure, finding a monetizable layer beneath the commodity embedding models

companies.one_liner: Paid embedding API launched 1 month after Google released BERT free

companies.name: Preflight

traction.peak_metric: ~20 employees and 'tens of customers' at time of acquisition (June 2023)

death_analysis.killer_competitor: Applitools (PE-backed by Thoma Bravo since March 2021, which consolidated the testing market and ultimately acquired Preflight as a capability fill)
death_analysis.death_mechanism: Preflight built a Chrome extension-based no-code testing platform that let non-engineers record, run, and maintain automated browser tests without writing Selenium code, targeting fast-growing startups plagued by brittle test suites. The company grew to approximately 20 employees but only acquired 'tens of customers' — implying an estimated ARR of $200K-$1.5M against an estimated annual burn of $2.5-3.5M. No Series A was raised in the 26-month window between its April 2021 seed round ($1.2M at $5M pre-money) and the June 2023 acquisition, indicating growth metrics never reached institutional thresholds. Applitools acquired Preflight on June 29, 2023 for a reported $10-15M (low confidence) — substantially an acqui-hire, as the CTO noted Preflight had the 'most robust AI abilities' among 'dozens of low code tools' evaluated.
death_analysis.critical_decision_point: After the $1.2M seed in April 2021, Preflight could have stayed lean at 5-8 employees instead of scaling to 20, extending runway by 2+ years while focusing on landing 3-5 anchor enterprise customers with $50K+ contracts. The headcount-to-customer ratio of 20 employees serving 'tens of customers' indicates the team scaled operations before achieving product-market fit, burning through capital that could have funded a longer search for repeatable enterprise demand.

similar_that_survived: Playwright (Microsoft) - open-source browser testing framework that commoditized the technical layer Preflight built on, but survived as a platform play inside Microsoft
Cypress - raised $55M+ for developer-focused end-to-end testing, winning by targeting developers directly rather than the no-code segment

companies.one_liner: 20 employees, tens of customers, acquired for ~$10-15M after 5 years

companies.name: Shiok Meats

traction.peak_metric: 100x cost reduction to $50/kg; 40+ employees; dedicated Singapore mini-plant; 35 investors across 6 rounds

death_analysis.killer_competitor: Conventional shrimp aquaculture's continuously improving cost structure and the 2022 alternative protein funding collapse
death_analysis.death_mechanism: Shiok Meats pioneered cultivated crustacean meat -- a category with zero prior scientific literature -- raising $30M across 6 rounds and achieving a 100x cost reduction from $5,000-7,000/kg to $50/kg over 2.5 years. But $50/kg was still 3-10x more expensive than commodity shrimp, and the fundamental biology of scaling crustacean stem cells proved intractable: CEO Sriram publicly confirmed in May 2023 that they could not scale cell lines into production. The planned 2023 Singapore commercial launch never materialized, the 40+ person team could not be sustained, and investor Agronomics had fully written down its position by June 2022. In March 2024, Shiok merged with Umami Bioworks in a share-for-share deal that effectively ended the company, with investors receiving limited or no return on $30M deployed.
death_analysis.critical_decision_point: In August 2021, Shiok acquired a 90%+ stake in Gaia Foods (cultivated red meat) and diversified into beef, pork, and mutton -- spreading already-scarce R&D resources across crustacean and mammalian cell biology simultaneously. Had they concentrated the remaining $10M Series A extension entirely on solving crustacean cell line scalability -- their core scientific bet -- they might have reached a definitive proof-of-concept or failure faster, either enabling a Series B raise before the 2022 funding collapse or preserving capital for a more orderly wind-down.

similar_that_survived: Upside Foods -- focused on cultivated chicken with better-understood cell biology, raised $600M+, and secured first-ever FDA/USDA approval for cultivated meat in 2023
Wildtype -- concentrated on cultivated salmon with a single-species focus, raised $100M+ and avoided diversification dilution

companies.one_liner: Spent $30M proving shrimp cells won't scale past $50/kg

companies.name: ZeroStorefront

traction.peak_metric: 2 named enterprise customers (Frank Pepe's, Halal Guys); broader count undisclosed

death_analysis.killer_competitor: DoorDash, Grubhub Direct (free competing tools); data withholding by delivery platforms
death_analysis.death_mechanism: ZeroStorefront built a restaurant data and marketing automation platform designed to unify fragmented delivery platform data, founded by two former Grubhub insiders who understood firsthand that DoorDash, Uber Eats, and Grubhub were keeping restaurants data-blind. The company raised only $225K-$450K across 4 rounds and operated with a 7-person team, while the delivery platforms it depended on for data had direct incentives to withhold it. In 2020, DoorDash and Grubhub launched free e-commerce tools for restaurants, commoditizing ZeroStorefront's lower-end functionality. With only 2 named customers (Frank Pepe's and Halal Guys) in the public record and an 18-month gap without any funding or press after August 2020, Thanx — already an investor — acquired ZeroStorefront in April 2022 to absorb its attribution and segmentation capabilities as a feature of Thanx's loyalty platform.
death_analysis.critical_decision_point: In 2020, when delivery platforms launched free restaurant e-commerce tools, ZeroStorefront should have abandoned the broad data aggregation play and focused exclusively on the attribution and customer identity layer that delivery platforms couldn't replicate — the exact capability Thanx later acquired them for. By trying to compete on the full stack (ordering, marketing, analytics) with 7 people and $450K, they diluted the one defensible asset they had.

similar_that_survived: Thanx - survived as a restaurant loyalty platform by owning the direct customer relationship and acquiring ZeroStorefront's data layer
Olo - survived by becoming the default digital ordering infrastructure for restaurant chains with deep POS integrations

companies.one_liner: Restaurant data platform with $450K vs. Grubhub's free tools

companies.name: Mini Exhibitions

traction.peak_metric: Enterprise clients included Amazon, Stripe, Spotify, and Y Combinator

death_analysis.killer_competitor: Return-to-office mandates (2022) — enterprise budgets redirected from virtual team events to in-person gatherings and hybrid office infrastructure
death_analysis.death_mechanism: Mini Exhibitions built a fully managed virtual events business for remote corporate teams — art tours, mixology classes, lockpicking workshops — shipping physical materials to distributed attendees and counting Amazon, Stripe, Spotify, and Y Combinator among its clients. The 2-person team operated on roughly $125K from YC with no additional disclosed funding, while the physical fulfillment model (shipping cocktail ingredients or clay to 50 employees per event) created per-event variable costs that compressed margins and capped the number of simultaneous events the team could manage. When return-to-office mandates accelerated through 2022 — Apple, Google, and Amazon all enforcing in-person policies — the people-ops budgets that had funded virtual engagement redirected to in-person events and office redesigns. Co-founder Sillings summarized the outcome in two words on his website: 'virtual events, dead.'
death_analysis.critical_decision_point: Mini Exhibitions remained exclusively virtual through 2022 even as return-to-office signals strengthened. Had the team pivoted to hybrid or in-person curated experiences for distributed teams — leveraging their enterprise client relationships with Amazon, Stripe, and Spotify — they could have captured the redirected people-ops budgets rather than losing them entirely.

similar_that_survived: Confetti — direct competitor in managed team events that survived by expanding to hybrid and in-person experiences as RTO accelerated
Teambuilding.com — diversified across virtual, hybrid, and in-person formats, maintaining relevance as the market shifted

companies.one_liner: $125K cocktail kits to Stripe & Amazon — RTO killed the market

companies.name: Orbiter

traction.peak_metric: $490K revenue in 2021 (unverified, Latka estimate)

death_analysis.killer_competitor: Monte Carlo (raised $236M, reached $1.6B valuation by May 2022 in the same data observability space)
death_analysis.death_mechanism: Orbiter built ML-powered business metric monitoring for non-engineers — a 'PagerDuty for the business side' — with a 3-person team of ex-Tesla, DoorDash, and Facebook operators. The company raised only ~$125K from YC W20 plus an undisclosed amount from Soma Capital, launching in March 2020 with a self-serve product requiring 5-minute onboarding and Slack-delivered anomaly alerts. An unverified third-party source reported $490K in 2021 revenue, suggesting early traction. But the data observability market consolidated rapidly around massively capitalized competitors: Monte Carlo raised $236M and hit a $1.6B valuation by May 2022, while Bigeye raised $66M by September 2021. Co-founder Mark Wai began co-founding Inkly in January 2021 while Orbiter was still operating — a clear signal of declining conviction — and the company wound down quietly around early 2022 with no acquisition or post-mortem.
death_analysis.critical_decision_point: After the March 2020 launch showed early product-market resonance on Hacker News, Orbiter could have aggressively pursued a seed round in Q2-Q3 2020 — when VC appetite for data infrastructure was surging — rather than continuing to operate lean on pre-seed capital. Raising $3-5M would have enabled the team to hire enterprise sales reps and build the connector library needed to compete before Monte Carlo and Bigeye locked up the market.

similar_that_survived: Monte Carlo — survived by raising $236M and building the enterprise-grade data observability platform with broad connector support and dedicated sales teams that Orbiter's 3-person team could not match
Metaplane — survived in data observability by raising $8.4M seed and focusing on the modern data stack (dbt, Snowflake, Looker) with a more targeted integration strategy

companies.one_liner: $125K vs Monte Carlo's $236M — capital asymmetry kills

companies.name: Quickcard

traction.peak_metric: 100+ upvotes on YC Bookface with 'tons of sign-ups' (2021)

death_analysis.killer_competitor: HubSpot Sales Hub and Salesforce AppExchange (which absorbed document tracking and personalization as native features, eliminating the need for a standalone point solution)
death_analysis.death_mechanism: Quickcard (originally Parsegon, then Battlecard) built tracked, personalized sales collateral that adapted to specific recipients and reported engagement data like opens and clicks. The 3-person team cycled through at least 4 distinct product directions — education, quantitative recruiting, sales playbook documentation, and personalized sales collateral — in under 2 years, resetting customer discovery each time. The strongest traction signal was 100+ upvotes on YC's internal Bookface platform, but no paying customer counts or revenue were ever disclosed. The founder acknowledged the core go-to-market challenge: 'people often slowly reject you until you die from a thousand cuts.' By late 2022, both founders had moved on without a formal shutdown announcement, and Tracxn confirmed the company as inactive in November 2024.
death_analysis.critical_decision_point: In July 2020, when the team was still committed to Battlecard (the sales playbook tool), they could have stayed the course rather than pivoting to Quickcard 8 weeks later. The playbook use case accumulated institutional knowledge over time — competitive intelligence, objection handling, messaging — creating natural switching costs that a document-sharing tool lacked. By choosing the lighter, more transactional use case, the team optimized for faster adoption at the cost of long-term defensibility.

similar_that_survived: Seismic - raised $400M+ for enterprise sales enablement by building deep content governance and compliance features that created switching costs Quickcard never achieved
DocSend (acquired by Dropbox for $165M) - focused narrowly on document tracking and sharing, reaching scale before CRM platforms absorbed the feature

companies.one_liner: 4 pivots in 2 years; 100 Bookface upvotes, 0 disclosed customers

companies.name: Fable

death_analysis.killer_competitor: Linear (launched native docs in 2022) and Notion (20M users by 2022) -- both absorbed the spec-to-ticket workflow Fable was built around
death_analysis.death_mechanism: Fable built a collaborative product specification editor with two-way sync to Jira, Linear, Productboard, and Asana, positioning itself as 'Figma for PMs.' The two-person team raised ~$500K from YC's Winter 2021 batch plus an undisclosed seed from angel investor Aaron Rankin. Despite launching on Product Hunt in April 2022 with a per-maker pricing model, Fable never disclosed revenue or paying customers, and its April 2022 launch explicitly stated paid features were still 'forthcoming' -- 18 months after founding. The fatal blow came from platform absorption: Linear shipped native documents in 2022, Atlassian deepened Confluence-Jira integration, and Notion reached 20 million users -- all replicating Fable's core two-way sync feature without needing a third-party tool. The company wound down in mid-to-late 2022 as its ~$500K runway expired.
death_analysis.critical_decision_point: In early 2022, before Linear shipped native docs, Fable could have pivoted from being a sync layer to building a proprietary product intelligence platform -- aggregating cross-tool data about what specs lead to successful engineering outcomes. This would have created a data moat that no single issue tracker could replicate, rather than offering a sync feature any platform could absorb in one sprint.

similar_that_survived: Notion - survived with 20M+ users by being the full-stack workspace rather than a sync layer
Linear - survived by building docs natively into its issue tracker, eliminating the need for a third-party spec tool
Productboard - raised $260M+ by owning the product management layer with customer feedback data, not just spec editing

companies.one_liner: Spec editor synced to Jira -- Linear shipped it natively in 2022

companies.name: Playmaker

traction.peak_metric: Founder's personal FPL Twitter account had ~20,000 followers (pre-company validation signal)

death_analysis.killer_competitor: WhatsApp (as the default FPL mini-league chat) and the FPL platform itself, which could have natively absorbed Playmaker's features at any time
death_analysis.death_mechanism: Playmaker built a mobile social layer for Fantasy Premier League's 8 million players, offering league-specific group chats, live match updates via Opta data feeds, and player statistics. With only $125K from YC as its sole institutional funding and a 5-person team in Copenhagen, the app never solved the cold-start problem: convincing entire FPL mini-leagues to abandon their existing WhatsApp groups required every member to download a new app simultaneously. By shutdown, the company's Facebook page had just 106 likes and LinkedIn had 151 followers, indicating near-zero organic growth beyond the founders' network. Playmaker entered liquidation in August 2021, roughly 6-7 months after completing YC W21, with no acquisition or acqui-hire.
death_analysis.critical_decision_point: Rather than building a standalone app requiring full mini-league adoption, Playmaker could have launched as a WhatsApp bot or integration that enriched existing group chats with live FPL scores, player stats, and league standings — meeting users where they already were. This would have eliminated the cold-start problem entirely and built distribution through existing group dynamics before migrating users to a dedicated platform.

similar_that_survived: FPL Twitter community accounts - built large audiences (100K+ followers) by layering FPL content on existing platforms rather than competing with them
Sleeper (fantasy sports app) - raised $40M+ and solved the cold-start problem by offering cross-sport fantasy leagues with real-money mechanics and integrated chat

companies.one_liner: 106 Facebook likes to replace 8M players' WhatsApp groups

companies.name: Queenly

traction.peak_metric: $97K GMV in March 2021; 1M registered users and 60,000+ dresses listed by July 2021

death_analysis.killer_competitor: Poshmark and eBay (horizontal resale platforms with tens of millions of active buyers that made Queenly's vertical approach structurally disadvantaged on distribution)
death_analysis.death_mechanism: Queenly built a peer-to-peer resale marketplace for prom, pageant, quinceañera, and wedding dresses, growing to 1 million registered users and raising $7.1M including a $6.3M seed extension led by Andreessen Horowitz (Connie Chan). At its peak in early 2021, the platform reported $97K GMV in a single month and claimed profitability at YC Demo Day. But by late 2023, the company was burning $273,833 per month against just $200,799 in annual revenue — spending 16x its annual revenue every month — with a transaction conversion rate of roughly 0.5% across its 1M users. A last-ditch Wefunder crowdfunding campaign at a $14M valuation cap (69.72x revenue multiple, flagged as overvalued by Kingscrowd) failed to change the trajectory, and the company shut down in late 2024.
death_analysis.critical_decision_point: After raising $7.1M in mid-2021 with strong early metrics ($97K monthly GMV, 125K users), Queenly could have focused exclusively on the pageant vertical — the highest-AOV, most repeat-purchase segment — rather than expanding into quinceañera (acquiring Mi Padrino in June 2022) and international markets (Canada in March 2023). Concentrating on the pageant niche with its built-in community (Miss USA, Miss Black USA partnerships already signed) could have driven the transaction density needed to reach sustainable unit economics before the capital ran out.

similar_that_survived: Poshmark - built a horizontal resale marketplace with social features, reaching $300M+ revenue by casting a wide category net rather than going vertical-only
StillWhite - survived as a niche wedding dress resale marketplace by staying extremely lean with minimal headcount and low burn

companies.one_liner: 1M users, $200K revenue, burning $274K/month — a16z-backed

companies.name: Bifrost

death_analysis.killer_competitor: 2022 crypto winter (BTC $69K to $16K, Terra/LUNA $40B wipeout, FTX collapse)
death_analysis.death_mechanism: Bifrost built smart contracts to automatically transfer crypto assets upon a holder's death, entering YC W22 with a $500K check and a 4-person team in San Francisco. Bitcoin peaked at $69K in November 2021 — the month they likely applied — then cratered to under $20K by June 2022, with the Terra/LUNA collapse erasing $40B in May and FTX imploding in November, destroying retail confidence in crypto-native financial products. The product demanded a double trust burden — trust a tiny startup with irreplaceable digital assets AND maintain long-term conviction in crypto — while competing against the default behavior of writing seed phrases on paper, and against exchanges like Coinbase that could add a beneficiary form as a single feature. With $500K funding a burn rate of $50K-$80K/month for only 6-10 months of runway, Bifrost hit its fundraising deadline in Q3-Q4 2022, precisely when investor appetite for crypto startups had collapsed, and quietly went inactive.
death_analysis.critical_decision_point: In Q1 2022, before Demo Day in March, the team could have pivoted to traditional estate planning with a crypto module rather than being crypto-native — targeting the $10B estate planning market with broader appeal. By going all-in on a crypto-only product, they tied their fate entirely to an asset class that lost 70% of its value within months of launch.

similar_that_survived: Trust & Will — digital estate planning without crypto dependency, raised $15M+
Casa — Bitcoin self-custody with inheritance planning feature, survived by serving broader security needs
Coinbase — could add beneficiary designation as a simple feature for 100M+ users

companies.one_liner: $500K runway × 70% crypto crash × 0 users = inactive

companies.name: CoffeeAI

traction.peak_metric: $30K ARR within 3 months of pivot launch; 269 Chrome Web Store ratings at 4.1/5 average

death_analysis.killer_competitor: Lavender (raised $13.2M Series A in 2023) — purpose-built AI sales writing Chrome extension with similar functionality, plus established players Outreach.io and Apollo.io adding AI features to existing platforms with millions of users
death_analysis.death_mechanism: CoffeeAI pivoted from Segna (a data wrangling platform) into AI-powered sales outreach at precisely the worst moment — just as ChatGPT's November 2022 release triggered a flood of near-identical Chrome extensions built on the same OpenAI API. The company reached $30K ARR within three months and accumulated 269 Chrome Web Store ratings at 4.1/5, but revenue growth stalled far below venture scale as dozens of free competitors flooded the Chrome Web Store. With roughly $1.38M in total funding (NZ$880K plus $500K from YC) and a 5-person team based in Auckland — 8,800 miles from the US sales teams they targeted — CoffeeAI lacked the enterprise integrations, proprietary data, or network effects to defend against commoditization. By 2024 both founders had moved on, with the irony that their abandoned product Segna operated in a market with far higher technical barriers to entry.
death_analysis.critical_decision_point: In late 2022, when considering the pivot from Segna to CoffeeAI, the founders chose to enter the AI sales outreach market using commoditized ChatGPT APIs rather than applying AI capabilities to enhance their existing data wrangling product. Had they instead built AI-powered data pipeline automation on top of Segna's technical foundation — a market with higher switching costs and fewer commodity competitors — they could have leveraged their engineering advantage in a defensible niche rather than competing against weekend-project Chrome extensions.

similar_that_survived: Lavender - survived by raising $13.2M and building deeper sales coaching features beyond simple message generation
Apollo.io - thrived by combining AI-powered outreach with a comprehensive B2B data platform and CRM, creating switching costs CoffeeAI lacked
Clay - survived by building a data enrichment and outreach platform with 75+ integrations creating genuine workflow lock-in

companies.one_liner: 269 Chrome reviews, $30K ARR — drowned in a sea of free ChatGPT clones

companies.name: Demo Gorilla

death_analysis.killer_competitor: Gong (raised $584M total) and Chorus (acquired by ZoomInfo for $575M) — both expanding from call analytics into pre-call coaching and deal intelligence, moving into Demo Gorilla's territory from above with massive enterprise distribution
death_analysis.death_mechanism: Demo Gorilla built a browser extension that surfaced AI-generated, persona-customized talking points for sales reps during live software demos, founded by two PagerDuty/Sentry.io veterans. The company raised only $500K from YC (W22) and entered a salestech market where competitors Gong and Chorus (acquired by ZoomInfo for $575M) had raised hundreds of millions, while adjacent players Highspot ($3.5B valuation) and Seismic ($3B valuation) were expanding into coaching and enablement. The browser extension delivery model created an enterprise adoption ceiling — IT departments routinely block extensions on managed devices — and the AI value proposition launched in early 2022, a full year before GPT-4 made reliable persona-specific content generation feasible. Without follow-on funding after Demo Day (despite a Wing VC callout), the company quietly wound down by 2023 with no public announcement, acquisition, or post-mortem.
death_analysis.critical_decision_point: At YC W22 Demo Day in Spring 2022, when Wing VC highlighted Demo Gorilla but no follow-on seed materialized, the founders could have pivoted from a standalone browser extension to an integration layer for existing platforms like Gong or Highspot. By building 'real-time demo coaching' as a plugin for platforms that already had enterprise IT approval and sales team adoption, they could have bypassed the browser extension adoption ceiling and leveraged incumbents' distribution rather than competing against it.

similar_that_survived: Navattic - survived by building interactive demo creation tools (rather than live demo assistance) and raising $11M+, avoiding the real-time delivery complexity
Reprise - survived in the demo experience space with $30M+ in funding and a product that worked outside the browser extension constraint
Walnut - survived by focusing on no-code interactive demo creation with $56M+ raised, targeting a broader use case than live demo coaching

companies.one_liner: $500K vs Gong's $584M — a browser extension can't storm the enterprise

companies.name: Flike

traction.peak_metric: 85 Product Hunt reviews with 4.8 rating; customers include Brex and Brightspot; doubled open rates reported by users

death_analysis.killer_competitor: Lavender ($13.2M raised), Apollo.io (275M+ contacts), Regie.ai ($20M+ raised) -- collectively saturated the AI sales email market before Flike's final pivot could gain traction
death_analysis.death_mechanism: Flike launched as 'GitHub for Spreadsheets' for finance teams, pivoted to an AI recommender engine (partnering with Mux), then pivoted again to an AI Sales Co-Pilot generating personalized outbound emails from 1,000+ prospect data points. The three-person founding team raised $2.1M total across a $1.6M pre-seed (March 2022) and additional funding, but two pivots consumed the majority of runway and reset go-to-market momentum each time. By the time the AI Sales Co-Pilot launched on Product Hunt in February 2024 with 85 reviews and a 4.8 rating, the market was saturated: Lavender had raised $13.2M, Regie.ai had raised $20M+, Apollo.io had 275M+ contacts, and Amplemarket had raised $12M. On June 14, 2024, fellow YC company FirstQuadrant acquired Flike in an asset deal -- a structure indicating the company had not achieved the revenue scale for a traditional acquisition.
death_analysis.critical_decision_point: In early 2022, after raising $1.6M for the spreadsheet collaboration product, the team could have committed to the finance vertical and iterated on product-market fit rather than pivoting twice. The 1.3 billion spreadsheet users represented a massive TAM, and staying focused would have preserved runway and brand equity instead of resetting go-to-market twice over 18 months.

similar_that_survived: Lavender - stayed focused on AI email coaching from the start, raised $13.2M, and built a large SDR community
Apollo.io - survived by combining a 275M+ contact database with AI email features, creating a data moat no seed-stage company could replicate
Causal (spreadsheet space) - raised $20M+ by staying focused on the financial modeling vertical Flike abandoned

companies.one_liner: 2 pivots on $2.1M, landed in a market with $45M+ in rivals

companies.name: Nophin

death_analysis.killer_competitor: GPT-4 (March 2023) and CRE platform incumbents (CoStar, CBRE) — LLM APIs commoditized Cresa's document parsing, while incumbents could add it as a feature to existing platforms
death_analysis.death_mechanism: Nophin entered YC W22 with a rental income advance platform for residential landlords, then pivoted mid-batch to Cresa, an AI deal screening analyst for multifamily commercial real estate acquisition teams. The pivot consumed runway on a capital base of approximately $1.88M raised from 11 investors including Liquid 2 Ventures and Goodwater Capital. Cresa's core value proposition — parsing rent rolls, T-12s, and offering memoranda — required fine-tuned NLP models that were expensive to build and brittle to maintain in 2022, exactly one year before GPT-4 would make the same workflow dramatically cheaper and more reliable. Neither product produced documented traction: no customer counts, no revenue figures, no press coverage emerged after the seed round closed in May 2022. By 2023, both the CEO and CTO had departed for new ventures, and the company appears to have quietly dissolved.
death_analysis.critical_decision_point: Nophin pivoted from rental income advances to CRE deal screening mid-YC batch, resetting the customer development clock entirely. Had the team committed to the original rental advance product and spent their $1.88M building landlord distribution and underwriting data, they could have established a defensible position in rent-tech before the pivot burned through 12+ months of runway on an unvalidated AI product in a completely different market.

similar_that_survived: Dealpath — CRE deal management platform that survived by raising larger rounds and building deep workflow integrations before AI commoditized document parsing
Blooma — AI-powered CRE underwriting tool that raised $25M+ by launching later when LLM infrastructure was mature enough to deliver reliable document parsing

companies.one_liner: $1.88M across 2 products in 2 markets — 0 traction disclosed

companies.name: Ozone
